title: Rice Portions Per Person Calculator | Hacé Cuentas
description: Calculate rice portions per person: 60-80g side dish, 100g main course. Fast & free calculator.
intro: This calculator helps you measure the right amount of dry uncooked rice to prepare when serving it as a side dish. The standard serving size for rice as a side dish is **¼ cup (45 g) of dry, uncooked rice per person**, which expands to roughly ¾ cup (180 g) of cooked rice after absorbing water. Knowing this ratio prevents both waste and shortage, whether you're cooking for 2 or 200. The USDA's FoodData Central lists a standard serving of cooked long-grain white rice at approximately 186 g (1 cup), but for a side dish—where rice shares the plate with a protein and a vegetable—the industry standard drops to ¾ cup cooked, making ¼ cup dry the correct starting point per guest.
keyTakeaway: **Side-dish rice: ¼ cup (45 g) dry → ¾ cup (180 g) cooked per person. Dry-to-cooked ratio ≈ 1:3 by volume. For a main course, double to ½ cup (90 g) dry per person.**
useCases: Planning a dinner party where rice is served alongside grilled chicken or steak and you need to buy the exact number of bags without over-purchasing.
Meal prepping a week's worth of rice side dishes for a household of 4, calculating the total dry rice needed to cook in one batch.
Catering or potluck coordination where you're assigned the rice side dish and must scale from a single serving to 25, 50, or 100 guests.
Restaurant or food-service staff portioning a hotel pan of cooked rice and back-calculating how many dry pounds were needed for inventory control.
explanation:
## How It's Calculated

The formula relies on two well-established constants: the dry serving size per person (for a side dish) and the dry-to-cooked expansion ratio.

```
Dry Rice Needed (cups) = Number of People × 0.25 cup

Cooked Rice Yielded (cups) = Dry Rice Needed × 3

Dry Rice Needed (grams) = Number of People × 45 g
Cooked Rice Yielded (grams) = Number of People × 135–180 g
```

The **1:3 volume expansion ratio** (1 cup dry → ~3 cups cooked) applies to long-grain white rice cooked by the absorption method (2 cups water per 1 cup rice). Brown rice expands slightly less (~1:2.5), and short-grain/sushi rice expands slightly more (~1:3.5). The calculator uses the long-grain white rice standard as its default baseline.

---

## Reference Table

| People | Dry Rice (cups) | Dry Rice (grams) | Dry Rice (oz) | Cooked Rice (cups) |
|--------|-----------------|------------------|---------------|---------------------|
| 1 | ¼ | 45 g | 1.6 oz | ¾ |
| 2 | ½ | 90 g | 3.2 oz | 1½ |
| 4 | 1 | 180 g | 6.3 oz | 3 |
| 6 | 1½ | 270 g | 9.5 oz | 4½ |
| 8 | 2 | 360 g | 12.7 oz | 6 |
| 10 | 2½ | 450 g | 15.9 oz | 7½ |
| 20 | 5 | 900 g | 31.7 oz (~2 lb) | 15 |
| 50 | 12½ | 2,250 g | ~5 lb | 37½ |
| 100 | 25 | 4,500 g | ~10 lb | 75 |

> **Tip for catering:** A standard 2 lb bag of long-grain white rice serves approximately 20 guests as a side dish.

---

## Typical Use Cases with Numbers

### Example 1 — Weeknight Dinner for 4
You're making baked salmon with rice. Using the formula: 4 × 0.25 = **1 cup of dry rice**. Add 2 cups of water, cook for 18 minutes, and you'll yield approximately 3 cups of fluffy cooked rice — about ¾ cup per plate.

### Example 2 — Backyard BBQ for 25 Guests
25 × 0.25 = **6.25 cups of dry rice** (roughly 1,125 g or ~2.5 lbs). You'd need to buy two standard 2 lb bags and have a small amount left over. Expected yield: ~18¾ cups of cooked rice.

### Example 3 — Weekly Meal Prep (1 Person, 5 Days)
5 days × 0.25 = **1.25 cups of dry rice** for the week. Cook all at once (yields ~3.75 cups cooked), divide into 5 containers of ¾ cup each, and refrigerate for up to 4 days or freeze the rest. The USDA recommends cooked rice be refrigerated within 2 hours and consumed within 3–4 days.

---

## Common Errors

1. **Measuring cooked rice instead of dry rice** — Many people see "¼ cup per person" and scoop cooked rice, ending up with only 25% of what's needed. Always measure *before* cooking.
2. **Ignoring the serving type (side vs. main)** — For a main course rice bowl, the standard doubles to ½ cup dry (90 g) per person. Using side-dish quantities for a main leaves guests hungry.
3. **Applying the 1:3 ratio to brown or wild rice** — Brown rice absorbs less water and yields closer to a 1:2.5 ratio, meaning you slightly underserve if you apply the white-rice formula blindly. Wild rice expands even more (up to 1:4).
4. **Forgetting buffer for large events** — For groups over 20, food service best practice is to add a 10–15% overage to account for uneven serving, plate spillage, and second helpings. For 50 guests, plan for 55 portions (13.75 cups dry).
5. **Not accounting for rice lost to rinsing** — Rinsing removes surface starch but also causes minor grain loss. For precision catering, weigh dry rice by grams rather than measuring by cup after rinsing.

---

- [Meal Prep Portions Calculator](/meal-prep-portions)
- [Food Cost Per Serving Calculator](/food-cost-per-serving)

faq.q: How much dry rice do I need per person for a side dish?
faq.a: The standard is ¼ cup (45 g) of dry, uncooked long-grain white rice per person when served as a side dish. This yields approximately ¾ cup (180 g) of cooked rice per serving — enough to accompany a protein and vegetable without overfilling the plate. The USDA FoodData Central lists one cup of cooked long-grain white rice at 206 calories, so the ¾-cup side dish comes in around 155 calories.

faq.q: How much rice do I need for 10 people as a side dish?
faq.a: For 10 people: 10 × 0.25 cup = 2.5 cups of dry rice (approximately 450 g or about 1 lb). This will yield roughly 7.5 cups of cooked rice. A standard 1 lb bag of long-grain white rice is almost exactly the right amount for 10 side-dish servings, with very little waste.

faq.q: What is the dry-to-cooked rice volume ratio?
faq.a: For long-grain white rice using the absorption method (2 cups water per 1 cup dry rice), the ratio is approximately 1:3 by volume — 1 cup dry produces about 3 cups cooked. Brown rice runs closer to 1:2.5, and short-grain or sushi rice can reach 1:3.5. By weight, 1 cup of dry long-grain white rice (~185 g) yields about 3 cups cooked (~555 g), as water is absorbed during cooking.

faq.q: How does a side-dish portion differ from a main-course portion?
faq.a: A side-dish portion is ¼ cup (45 g) dry rice per person, yielding ¾ cup cooked. A main-course portion — such as a rice bowl, biryani, or fried rice — is typically ½ cup (90 g) dry per person, yielding 1½ cups cooked. Using side-dish amounts for a main course will consistently leave guests under-served, especially if there is no accompanying bread or other starch.

faq.q: How many servings does a standard 2 lb bag of rice make as a side dish?
faq.a: A 2 lb (907 g) bag of long-grain white rice contains approximately 20 quarter-cup dry servings. At ¼ cup dry per person, a 2 lb bag feeds exactly 20 guests as a side dish. For a main course (½ cup dry), that same bag serves only 10. Most commercial rice bags label servings as ¼ cup dry, so the number of servings printed on the package aligns with side-dish portions.

faq.q: Can I use the same formula for brown rice or jasmine rice?
faq.a: The ¼ cup dry per person starting point stays the same for all rice types. However, the cooked yield differs: brown rice yields ~2.5× its dry volume (slightly less fluffy), while jasmine rice yields ~3× and has a slightly stickier texture. Wild rice can yield up to 4× its dry volume. When cooking brown rice, also note it requires more water (2.5 cups water per 1 cup dry) and a longer cook time (~45 minutes vs. 18 minutes for white rice).

faq.q: How long can leftover cooked rice be safely stored?
faq.a: According to USDA food safety guidelines, cooked rice should be refrigerated within 2 hours of cooking (1 hour if the ambient temperature exceeds 90°F / 32°C). Properly refrigerated cooked rice is safe to eat for 3–4 days. For longer storage, freeze cooked rice in airtight containers for up to 6 months. Reheating must bring rice back to an internal temperature of 165°F (74°C) to eliminate any Bacillus cereus bacteria risk.

faq.q: How much water do I need when cooking the calculated amount of rice?
faq.a: For long-grain white rice, the standard ratio is 2 cups of water per 1 cup of dry rice. So if you're cooking 1 cup of dry rice (4 servings), use 2 cups of water. For 2.5 cups dry rice (10 servings), use 5 cups of water. Brown rice needs 2.5 cups of water per 1 cup dry. These ratios assume a covered pot on the stovetop; rice cookers and Instant Pots may require slightly less water due to reduced evaporation.

sources.name: USDA FoodData Central – Cooked Long-Grain White Rice Nutrition
USDA Food Safety – Leftovers and Food Safety

dataUpdate.notes: Fixed formula, no external data updates required
